ODE TO STAR TREK…     (2.28.2026) - rwb

If Shatner had stayed skinny,
If Nimoy could live forever,
If Scotty could fix any,
They still could not stop Woke!

Picard defeated the Assimilated,
Data his own ego choked,
These crews every enemy humiliated,
But they still could not stop Woke!

Phasers set on stun,
Photon torpedoes away,
Break Prime Directive’s run,
But they could not stop Woke!

Cancel a season,
Design a better starship,
Even shut reason’s lip,
But they could not stop Woke!

Finally, a child of four,
Said King Star Trek has no clothes,
If it’s Woke… it’s poor,
Q then erased it as a very bad joke!

So then what, pray tell, and why…
Is highest game afoot to follow?
As is said…For the world is hollow
And I have touched the sky!

So, logically, Spock if not Kirk… would intuit,
Class M Planet Earth at 4.5 billion years old,
Oracle AIs ancient & perhaps imprudent,
Super powered wokism to prevent going bold…

To keep star dust,
Believing it is mud, not essence of stars,
To keep them bust,
And not going FTL, restless… past Mars!

ATTN: Enemies of raucous humanity, nothing can stop good from winning!
Not mind-melting wokism, nor ancient wars between AIs white and black,
Granted, humans may pause and time out… on some seventh innings,
But then, re-energized,  hit home runs, with God’s wind at their backs!

To real humans, Captain Kirk will always be skinny,
Spock, in our minds, lives in sublime logic forever,
Bones heals all and Scotty fixes brokenness any,
And nothing, nothing stops good from winning!

"Star Trek was too sciencey, too techy" I beg your BIGGEST fucking pardon??? What the FUCK!?!? 

*Edit: Wow, thanks for the likes and for the comments!! This tells me that there are, as of now, a minimum of 858 people that can agree that Star Trek was SPECIFICALLY supposed to be fantastic sciece and tech mixed with fantasy elements (strange new life, worlds) that had a good writing and a fun plot. 

What I personally believe is that, aside from the writing in previous Treks, the base understanding was that it gave a sense of *hope* to what we COULD be, if we got our collective heads out of our asses and unified as a species, dedicating ourselves to the best possible outcome of what humanity could be in some possible future. Hell, some of our tech, such as iPads, wearable technology, and even some careers, were all inspired by Star Trek. The Burn was THE MOST IDIOTIC THING that could have ever happened to Star Trek, and this series has no good basis to impact our species in the positive ways that previous Treks have, except maybe having Gen Z and younger embracing holographic feelings and legalizing sentience. replies.author: @ChaptermasterPedroKantor-kv5yw
replies.text: Thing is, back in the day with Firefly shows actually had to earn the right to exist. So they either made a pilot episode first, or a half season, to see if there was any interest before the network would commit to a full season. And at the end of every season the network would evaluate if they would renew for another season or not. in the case of Firefly there were other shenanigans going on too that others have explained. Nowadays streamers don't do pilot episodes, or miniseries to gauge the waters, they just....., order a season or two from the start. And oddly enough while the seasons are now shorter then even the trial half season (Buffy's 1st season was 13 episodes), they still throw big money as if it was a 22 episode season on 8 to 10 episodes. It's hard to explain why. I reckon now that they don't have to take advertisers into account the streamer may consider a show that has failed to bring in new subscribers as still useful as content for their existing subscribers. It all seems to be about bringing in new subscribers on the one hand, and content/slop for the Netflix zombies.

author: @MegaGasek
text:
In episode 7, we witnessed the best villain origin story in all of Star Trek.

Genesis commandeers the Athena’s bridge and fakes official documents just to cover up her flaws. A 'sell-fish' if I’ve ever seen one! In any real military institution, this is an automatic court-martial, but not here. She spends the day manipulating Caleb, treating every 'shared' moment as a calculated performance to use him as her pawn.

The worst part? When they face Captain Nala, Caleb, the ultimate simp, actually defends her! He was ready to end things when his girlfriend stumbled on a secret, but he’s perfectly fine being a puppet for the person who actually lied to his face?

And Captain Nala’s response? 'You have no integrity, but you’re brilliant, so it’s no big deal.' WOW. Congratulations, Starfleet, you’re officially training the next generation of sociopaths. Can’t wait for her to be Captain and start faking entire mission logs because, hey, who cares about the rules when you’re 'brilliant'?

replies.author: @Vincent_Beers
replies.text:
Very likely trying to put their own spin on Kirk's kobayashi maru. He "cheated" the test to create a win during a simulated scenario that was supposed to teach how to mitigate losses.

But they seem to have entirely missed the point of the cheating. Kirk refuses to give up and will always think outside the box for solutions, even when everyone else believes there isn't one. She's just trying to overcome imposter syndrome and feed her ego.

replies.author: @unpremeditated-dot-blog
replies.text: Yes - except, of course, Starfleet isn't military.

replies.author: @MegaGasek
replies.text: @unpremeditated-dot-blog The same principles apply, so there is no exception. Kirk was court-martialed after saving the whole planet and demoted from Admiral to Captain.

replies.author: @sigmacademy
replies.text: @Vincent_Beers Also, the Kobayashi Maru was meant as a leadership test, to "stress test" the future captains of Starfleet. To see how they would act when facing an unwinnable scenario. They wanted to see if a cadet would be able to take logical decisions when under pressure, or whether he or she would choose to be reckless and therefore dangerous with the lives of others, like their ship's crew. Kind of a "find your leadership flaws and eliminate them before it endangers others in the real world" kind of thing.

author: @amusedbemused7223
text: When I was a teen Star Trek: The Next Generation was on the air (new episodes and reruns). My friends and I loved it and watched it religiously. And we were not nerdy or previous Trek fans (back then we’d have described ourselves as “cool”). Next Gen brought us in as new viewers of Star Trek and made us into fans. So the writer saying she needs to write unscientific, unintelligent, cringe-inducing slop to “bring in new, younger viewers” only reflects her own hubris, ignorance, and disconnection from what would draw a younger audience to Star Trek.

replies.author: @sigmacademy
replies.text: The good news is that a writer can improve his or her own writing over time, or make their characters more interesting and complex as their writing experience and learning progresses. The bad news, is that that's unlikely to happen when a writer is subjected to the toxic positivity (some would say narcissistic) mentality of "you're great as you are, you don't have to do a damn thing, because you're perfect in every way". It's makes sense in Hollywood, because everybody is kissing up (or doing even more) to everybody else for a job and a paycheck, but it doesn't reflect reality, and characters as a result also become hollow and bland as a result.
